what_it_does: Converts a text reply into clean, engine-aware spoken prose by stripping markdown, code, URLs, emoji, and structural markers, then mapping a canonical set of inline expression tags to the selected TTS engine's supported syntax or dropping unsupported tags.
observations: Single universal function `speakable(reply, engine, warn=...)` intended as the pre-TTS seam for every voice path.
Strips fenced code, inline code, links/images, headings, blockquotes, tables, lists, emphasis, bare URLs, and emoji via an ordered regex pipeline.
Uses an engine registry `ENGINE_EXPRESSION` to render canonical `<tag>` expression markup into engine-native syntax; unknown engines strip all tags.
Canonical tag vocabulary is engine-agnostic and angle-bracketed, matching Orpheus syntax.
stdlib-only (`re`), avoiding third-party markdown libraries because the module is imported in both a Python 3.12 voice venv and a 3.14 runtime bridge.
Fail-loud: raises `TypeError` for non-string input and `ValueError` when a non-empty reply cleans to empty silence.
Unknown engine does not raise; it strips expression markup and surfaces a warning through an optional `warn` callback.
Cleaning is whole-reply rather than per-chunk so markdown structures are not split across chunk boundaries.
Whitespace normalization collapses newlines to spaces and trims stray control glyphs and excessive punctuation.
standouts: Engine-agnostic canonical expression tags mapped at synth time through a registry.
Whole-reply cleaning before sentence splitting to avoid chunk-boundary markdown corruption.
Fail-loud refusal to synthesize silence when a non-empty reply becomes empty after cleaning.
conventions: Registry-is-truth for engine expression capability mapping.
Fail-loud for malformed/silent data, graceful degradation for unknown engine configuration.
Single universal seam: one function for every reply-to-speech path.
Explicit ordered regex pipeline with inline comments explaining each step.
Type hints and docstrings on public and private functions.
Nested closure `repl` inside `_apply_expression` captures engine capabilities.
concerns: Markdown stripping regex is hand-rolled and may be brittle for nested or edge-case markdown (e.g. emphasis inside code, nested lists, complex tables).
`_EMOJI_RE` is defined twice; the second definition overwrites the first, leaving a stale comment about general punctuation that no longer matches the code.
Whitespace normalization turns all newlines into single spaces, potentially losing paragraph pauses.
The claim that markdown stripping does not touch `<...>` expression tags is a maintenance dependency; adding HTML stripping later would break it.
No tests or examples are visible in the file.
Unknown-engine warning is only emitted when expression tags are present, so a tag-free reply gives no signal that the engine is unrecognized.
notes: A pre-TTS text sanitizer and expression-tag mapper that makes any brain reply safe to speak on any supported engine.
questions: Where is the authoritative supported-engine list maintained beyond this file, given engines like qwen3tts/xtts/kokoro are mentioned but absent from `ENGINE_EXPRESSION`?
How are canonical expression tags injected into replies by the brain or persona—is there a prompt-level contract?
How does the bridge deliver `warn` messages to users or logs?
Are there integration tests verifying rendered output for each registered engine?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose—one universal reply-to-speech sanitizer with engine-aware expression tags—matches what the code implements.
intention_signals: It is trying to prevent TTS engines from reading markdown, code, URLs, and emoji literally while preserving intended paralinguistic expression through a small canonical tag vocabulary that can be adapted per engine.
novelty: The canonical expression-tag vocabulary with per-engine rendering registry, combined with a fail-loud refusal to synthesize cleaned-to-silence replies, makes this an architectural seam rather than a simple text cleaner.
contribution: A reusable, runtime-portable text-to-speech preparation layer that separates content cleaning from engine-specific expression syntax, so any reply channel can produce speakable output without duplicating markdown-stripping logic.
summary_for_embedding: voice/speakable.py is a stdlib-only pre-TTS text sanitizer that strips markdown, code, URLs, and emoji and maps canonical inline expression tags to engine-specific syntax before speech synthesis.
intention_for_embedding: To provide a single, engine-agnostic reply-to-speech normalization point that prevents TTS engines from reading non-spoken markup aloud while preserving supported paralinguistic cues.

symbols.does: Strips markdown structure and non-spoken content from text.
symbols.description: Uses an ordered regex pipeline to remove fenced and inline code, links and images, headings, blockquotes, horizontal rules, tables, list markers, emphasis, bare URLs, and emoji, returning the remaining spoken words.

symbols.does: Collapses leftover whitespace and stray punctuation into natural spoken spacing.
symbols.description: Removes decorative bullet and control glyphs, caps repeated punctuation at three occurrences, flattens newlines and multiple spaces into single spaces, and trims spaces around sentence punctuation.

symbols.does: Maps canonical inline expression tags to the selected engine's syntax or drops unsupported ones.
symbols.description: Scans text for canonical angle-bracket tags, looks up the engine in `ENGINE_EXPRESSION`, renders supported tags in engine-native syntax, and drops unsupported tags so they are never read aloud; returns the transformed text plus any warning about an unknown engine.

symbols.does: Per-match renderer or dropper for a canonical expression tag.
symbols.description: Nested closure inside `_apply_expression` that decides whether a matched canonical tag should be rendered for the current engine or removed, using the engine registry's `supports` and `render` entries.

symbols.does: Universal transform from a brain reply to clean, engine-aware speakable prose.
symbols.description: Validates input is a string, strips markdown, applies engine-specific expression tag mapping, normalizes whitespace, and raises if a non-empty reply cleans to nothing; optionally surfaces warnings through a caller-provided `warn` callback.
